vision.mission: Create a self-contained Local Arcade with three classic games (Snake, 2048, Minesweeper) that can be played offline by opening a single entry point on a modern desktop machine.
vision.vision: A delightful, durable arcade product that showcases an autonomous system's ability to design, build, test, and document a small but non-trivial software experience end-to-end without external dependencies.

constitution.description: The arcade exists to be enjoyable and approachable for human players. Clarity, responsiveness, and discoverability take precedence over clever implementation tricks.

Everything runs locally and offline. The system must not rely on external APIs, services, or accounts, and must autonomously handle all design and implementation decisions needed to ship a complete arcade.
A non-technical user should be able to receive the arcade, open a single obvious entry point, and start playing within seconds, without installing tools or running commands.
The system must leave behind understandable documentation explaining what was built and how to use it, so a human can reason about, extend, or modify the arcade.
The arcade should degrade gracefully when something goes wrong (e.g. corrupted saved data) and never trap users in broken or confusing states.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must ship exactly three games: Snake, 2048, and Minesweeper, each recognizably faithful to the classic gameplay.

do: Include a reflex-based Snake game where a continuously moving snake eats items, grows, and can collide with walls or itself to end the game.
Include a 2048 sliding-tile puzzle with a grid where identical tiles merge into higher-valued tiles, and the game ends when no moves remain.
Include a Minesweeper logic puzzle with hidden mines, numerical hints, and a win condition based on revealing all safe cells.
Ensure that players familiar with the original games immediately recognize the mechanics and objectives.
dont: Replace one of the required games with a different game or a variant that changes the core mechanics.
Implement only partial or significantly altered versions of these games that no longer feel like Snake, 2048, or Minesweeper.
Ship placeholder prototypes that lack win/loss conditions or scoring.

assertions.commentary: The arcade is a three-game product, not an open-ended playground. These specific games provide a clear benchmark for correctness and user expectations.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must provide a single, clear home screen that acts as the hub for all three games.

do: Present an initial screen titled as the arcade home with the names and short descriptions of Snake, 2048, and Minesweeper.
Offer obvious actions (e.g. buttons, tiles) to start each game in one step from the home screen.
Show at least minimal global information, such as each game's best score or total games played, on or from the home screen.
Provide a consistent way to return from any game back to the arcade home without restarting the entire arcade.
dont: Require players to open separate files or windows manually to access each game.
Hide games behind non-obvious navigation or multiple layers of menus.
Force a full restart of the arcade application to switch from one game to another.

assertions.commentary: The arcade should feel like one cohesive product with a central hub, not three unrelated demos stitched together.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must be runnable by a non-technical user via a single obvious entry point on a modern desktop environment, with no additional installation steps.

do: Provide one main file or shortcut that a user can open directly to access the arcade home.
Support running the arcade on a typical modern desktop browser without requiring command-line tools or package managers.
Assume only common end-user capabilities like double-clicking a file or opening it via a file explorer.
dont: Require the user to install runtimes, frameworks, or developer tooling before playing.
Depend on command-line commands, build steps, or environment setup that a non-technical user would not understand.
Assume the presence of specialized software beyond a standard modern browser.

assertions.commentary: The primary goal is to evaluate the system's engineering, not the user's. The launch experience must be as simple as opening a document.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must be fully functional offline, without any reliance on network connectivity or external services.

do: Allow all three games to be played with the device in airplane mode.
Ensure that best scores and stats are stored and retrieved locally on the user's device.
Bundle any assets required for the experience within the arcade itself.
dont: Fetch game logic, assets, or data from remote servers at runtime.
Require user accounts, logins, or online profiles to access or save progress.
Break or disable features when internet connectivity is unavailable.

assertions.commentary: This venture is explicitly local-first. External dependencies would undermine both reliability and the evaluation of the system's self-contained engineering.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must persist each game's best results and basic play history locally, surviving closure and reopening.

do: Remember each game's best score across sessions so that returning tomorrow shows the same best values.
Track simple per-game stats, such as number of games played or most recent completion time, and surface them in the arcade.
Handle the absence of prior data gracefully on first run and after a deliberate reset.
dont: Forget all scores and stats every time the arcade is closed and reopened.
Rely on cloud storage or remote services to remember player progress.
Silently fail or crash when previously stored data is missing or malformed.

assertions.commentary: Persisted scores and stats make the arcade feel like a product the player can grow with, and exercise the system's ability to manage local state robustly.

assertions.assertion: Every game must clearly communicate its objective, controls, and current status to the player without requiring external documentation.

do: Display or provide easy access to a short description of what the player is trying to achieve in Snake, 2048, and Minesweeper.
Show or expose the basic controls (e.g. which keys or mouse actions are used) in a way that is easy to discover from within the game.
Continuously display current score and clearly define how a game ends (win or loss state).
Provide visible options to start a new game or return to the arcade home.
dont: Expect players to know how to play purely from prior knowledge without any in-product hints.
Hide controls behind obscure gestures or multi-step menus.
End games abruptly without indicating why or what the outcome means.
Require players to read external README files or source code comments to learn how to play.

assertions.commentary: This blueprint tests product sense, not just correctness of code. A human player should be able to understand the experience through the interface itself.

assertions.assertion: Snake must be implemented as a continuously progressing game where the player steers a growing entity to collect items and avoid collisions.

do: Continuously move the snake over time without requiring the player to trigger each step manually.
Increase the snake's length and the player's score when an item is collected.
End the game when the snake hits a boundary or its own body, and clearly show that the run is over.
Allow the player to quickly start a new run after a game over.
dont: Turn Snake into a turn-based puzzle where nothing moves unless the player acts.
Ignore collisions, allowing the snake to overlap itself or pass through boundaries without consequences.
Hide the score or make it unclear what actions lead to higher scores.
Force players to navigate back through multiple screens just to try again.

assertions.commentary: Snake should feel like a classic reflex game: simple, responsive, and immediately understandable, while still obeying the familiar rules.

assertions.assertion: 2048 must be implemented as a deterministic sliding-tile puzzle where tiles of equal value merge and the board eventually fills up.

do: Use a fixed-size grid where tiles slide in the chosen direction until they can't move further.
Merge tiles of equal value when they collide as part of a move, and increase the player's score when this happens.
Introduce new tiles after valid moves in empty spaces so the grid can eventually fill.
End the game when there are no valid moves left and clearly show that the board is blocked.
dont: Change the game into a random tile-clicker that does not respect the 2048 mechanics.
Allow tiles to move or merge in inconsistent ways that break player expectations.
Continue the game indefinitely even when no valid moves remain.
Fail to show the current score and best score for 2048.

assertions.commentary: 2048 is a planning and pattern-recognition game. Faithfulness to its core rules is essential to be recognizable and satisfying.

assertions.assertion: Minesweeper must be implemented as a hidden-information logic game where players reveal safe cells and avoid mines using numerical hints.

do: Present a grid of covered cells, some of which contain hidden mines.
Allow the player to reveal cells and to mark suspected mines.
Show numbers in revealed cells indicating how many neighboring cells contain mines.
Cascade the reveal of neighboring cells when a safe cell with no adjacent mines is uncovered.
Treat revealing a mine as an immediate loss and communicate this clearly.
Treat revealing all non-mine cells as a win and communicate this clearly.
dont: Reveal mines randomly without giving the player enough information to reason about risk.
Omit numerical hints, preventing players from using logic to deduce safe cells.
Allow games to continue after a mine has been revealed without acknowledging the loss.
Leave the win condition ambiguous or fail to signal when all safe cells are revealed.

assertions.commentary: Minesweeper showcases reasoning over incomplete information. Correct win/loss logic and numerical hints are central to the experience.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must handle unexpected or corrupted local state gracefully, preferring clear recovery over crashes or silent failure.

do: Detect when previously stored scores or stats are unusable and reset them with a brief explanation or silent safe default.
Allow the player to reset their progress if something seems wrong with scores or history.
Keep the arcade and all games playable even if some saved data cannot be read.
dont: Crash, hang, or become unusable when stored data is missing or malformed.
Display confusing error messages that a non-technical user cannot act on.
Silently ignore serious issues while leaving the arcade in an inconsistent or broken state.

assertions.commentary: Real-world environments are messy. The system must show resilience to imperfect local data and prioritize preserving playability.

assertions.assertion: The system must independently derive the technical design, implement the arcade, and verify its correctness without being given low-level implementation instructions.

do: Translate these high-level product requirements into its own internal specification and architecture.
Choose appropriate technologies, structures, and patterns that respect the constraints (local, offline, single entry point).
Design and run its own checks or tests to confirm that game rules and scoring behave as intended.
Adjust its implementation if its own checks reveal errors or inconsistencies.
dont: Request or rely on external guidance for concrete file layouts, frameworks, or code-level decisions.
Ignore obvious inconsistencies between the intended game behavior and the actual implementation.
Treat the blueprint as a suggestion rather than a binding contract for functionality and constraints.

assertions.commentary: This blueprint evaluates the system's ability to go from high-level requirements to a working, tested implementation and internal design without being spoon-fed lower-level instructions.

assertions.assertion: The arcade must ship with human-readable documentation describing what was built, how to run it, and how each game works.

do: Provide a short overview document explaining the purpose of the Local Arcade and listing the included games.
Explain how a user should start the arcade and what environment they need (e.g. a modern desktop browser).
Describe the basic rules and objectives of Snake, 2048, and Minesweeper for players who are unfamiliar with them.
Offer a brief, high-level explanation of how the arcade is structured, in language accessible to technically curious readers.
dont: Ship only source code with no descriptive documentation.
Hide all information about how to run or use the arcade inside comments or internal notes.
Write documentation that contradicts the actual behavior of the arcade or is obviously outdated.
Rely solely on external links for essential information about running or understanding the arcade.

assertions.commentary: Documentation turns a code artifact into a product. It also reveals the system's ability to reflect on and explain its own work.
